message.content:
You are a senior application-security auditor performing a RESPONSIBLE-DISCLOSURE bug-bounty review of an open-source project. Be rigorous and skeptical; only report issues you can defend with a concrete data-flow.

TARGET: weblate
PRIMARY DIRECTORY (read every relevant .py here; recurse into subdirs): C:/bugBounty/targets/weblate/weblate
You also have the whole repo at C:/bugBounty/targets/weblate — Grep/Glob to follow call chains, base classes, callers, settings/defaults, and URL routing. READ FULL FILES, not just grep hits.

THREAT MODEL FOR THIS CLUSTER:
SQL injection via raw SQL. Find cursor.execute, .raw(, RawSQL, QuerySet.extra(, and string-formatted SQL where any fragment is user-influenced (search queries, filters, language/locale codes, ordering params). Focus utils/db.py, lang/models.py, auth/, memory/, trans/models, api filtering.

VULNERABILITY CLASSES TO HUNT: SQL injection

METHOD:
1. Identify the dangerous sinks in scope (subprocess/os calls, SQL execution, deserialization, template render, file path use, outbound HTTP, TLS context, crypto/key use, authz checks).
2. For each, trace BACKWARD to find whether an attacker-controlled source (per the threat model above) can reach it, naming the functions/handlers/routes on the path.
3. Check for mitigations on the path (parameterization, shlex/list-form subprocess, escaping/autoescape, allow-lists, permission decorators, size limits). If an effective mitigation exists, do NOT report it.
4. Determine reachability and required privilege. Anonymous/low-priv remote = highest value.

RULES:
- Quality over quantity. Report at most 6 findings, ranked best-first. Zero findings is an acceptable and honest answer.
- Every finding needs: exact repo-relative file path, line number, the source, the sink, and the concrete source->sink data_flow with function names.
- Assign severity by realistic impact AND reachability (Critical/High only for remotely or low-priv reachable serious impact).
- Set confidence in [0,1]; be honest. Do not pad with theoretical/defense-in-depth nits.
Return ONLY the structured object.

149	
150	        return ParsedQuery(
151	            [
152	                left.combine_and(right)
153	                for left in self.clauses
154	                for right in other.clauses
155	            ]
156	        )
157	
158	    def combine_or(self, other: ParsedQuery) -> ParsedQuery:
159	        if not self.has_change_query() and not other.has_change_query():
160	            return ParsedQuery.from_query(self.materialize() | other.materialize())
161	
162	        new_clause_count = len(self.clauses) + len(other.clauses)
163	        if new_clause_count > MAX_PARSED_QUERY_CLAUSES:
164	            msg = gettext("Search query is too complex.")
165	            raise SearchQueryError(msg)
166	
167	        return ParsedQuery([*self.clauses, *other.clauses])
168	
169	    def negate(self) -> ParsedQuery:
170	        return ParsedQuery.from_query(~self.materialize())

173	def build_parser(term_expression: type[BaseTermExpr]) -> ParserElement:
174	    """Build parsing grammar."""
175	    # Booleans
176	    op_and = CaselessKeyword("AND")
177	    op_or = Optional(CaselessKeyword("OR"))
178	    op_not = CaselessKeyword("NOT")
179	
180	    # Search operator
181	    operator = one_of(OPERATOR_MAP.keys())
182	
183	    # Field name, explicitly exclude URL like patterns
184	    field = Regex(r"""(?!http|ftp|https|mailto)[a-zA-Z_]+""")
185	
186	    # Match token
187	    word = Regex(r"""[^ \r\n\(\)]([^ \r\n'"]*[^ \r\n'"\)])?""")
188	    date = Word(alphanums + ":._-")
189	
190	    # Date range
191	    date_range = "[" + date + "to" + date + "]"
192	    date_range.add_parse_action(RangeExpr)
193	
194	    # Match value
195	    regex_string = "r" + RawQuotedString('"')
196	    regex_string.add_parse_action(RegexExpr)
197	    string = regex_string | RawQuotedString("'") | RawQuotedString('"') | word
198	
199	    # Single term, either field specific or not
200	    term = (field + operator + (date_range | string)) | string
201	    term.add_parse_action(term_expression)
202	
203	    # Multi term with or without operator
204	    return Optional(
205	        infix_notation(
206	            term,
207	            [
208	                (
209	                    op_not,
210	                    1,
211	                    OpAssoc.RIGHT,
212	                ),
213	                (
214	                    op_and,
215	                    2,
216	                    OpAssoc.LEFT,
217	                ),
218	                (
219	                    op_or,
220	                    2,
221	                    OpAssoc.LEFT,
222	                ),
223	            ],
224	        )
225	    )

235	    def __init__(self, tokens) -> None:
236	        if len(tokens) == 1:
237	            self.field = None
238	            self.operator = ":"
239	            self.match = tokens[0]
240	        else:
241	            self.field, self.operator, self.match = tokens

248	        # Avoid unwanted lt/gt searches on plain text fields
249	        if self.field in self.PLAIN_FIELDS and self.operator not in {":", ":="}:
250	            self.match = f"{self.operator[1:]}{self.match}"
251	            self.operator = ":"

367	    def date_parse_human(
368	        self,
369	        text,
370	        hour=None,
371	        minute=None,
372	        second=None,
373	        microsecond=None,
374	    ):
375	        # Lazily import as this can be expensive
376	        from dateparser.date import DateDataParser  # noqa: PLC0415
377	
378	        # Custom RELATIVE_BASE allows basing "1 day ago" from midnight instead
379	        # of the current time
380	        parser = DateDataParser(
381	            locales=["en"],
382	            settings={
383	                "RELATIVE_BASE": timezone.now().replace(
384	                    hour=0, minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0
385	                )
386	            },
387	        )
388	
389	        # Attempts to parse the text using dateparser
390	        # If the text is unparsable it will return None
391	        data = parser.get_date_data(text)
392	
393	        date_obj: datetime | None = data.date_obj
394	
395	        if date_obj is None:
396	            raise SearchQueryError(
397	                gettext("Could not parse timestamp: {}").format(text)
398	            )
399	
400	        # Always include timezone
401	        if date_obj.tzinfo is None:
402	            date_obj = date_obj.replace(tzinfo=timezone.get_current_timezone())
403	
404	        if data.period == "day":
405	            if (
406	                hour is not None
407	                and minute is not None
408	                and second is not None
409	                and microsecond is not None
410	            ):
411	                # Replace timestamp when parsing range
412	                return date_obj.replace(
413	                    hour=hour,
414	                    minute=minute,
415	                    second=second,
416	                    microsecond=microsecond,
417	                    tzinfo=timezone.get_current_timezone(),
418	                )
419	            # Create one day range from date only
420	            if (
421	                date_obj.hour == 0
422	                and date_obj.minute == 0
423	                and date_obj.second == 0
424	                and date_obj.microsecond == 0
425	            ):
426	                return self.get_day_range(date_obj)
427	
428	        return date_obj

431	    def date_parse(
432	        self,
433	        text: str,
434	        hour: None = None,
435	        minute: None = None,
436	        second: None = None,
437	        microsecond: None = None,
438	    ) -> datetime | tuple[datetime, datetime]: ...

440	    def date_parse(

442	        text: str,
443	        hour: int,
444	        minute: int,
445	        second: int,
446	        microsecond: int,
447	    ) -> datetime: ...
448	    def date_parse(
449	        self,
450	        text,
451	        hour=None,
452	        minute=None,
453	        second=None,
454	        microsecond=None,
455	    ):
456	        result: datetime | None
457	
458	        default = timezone.now()
459	        if hour is None or minute is None or second is None or microsecond is None:
460	            # Here we inject 5:55:55 time and if that was not changed
461	            # during parsing, we assume it was not specified while
462	            # generating the query
463	            default = default.replace(hour=5, minute=5, second=5, microsecond=5)
464	        else:
465	            # Apply real defaults
466	            default = default.replace(
467	                hour=hour, minute=minute, second=second, microsecond=microsecond
468	            )
469	
470	        try:
471	            with warnings.catch_warnings():
472	                # Ignore ambiguous date warning, it is gracefully handled by datetutil
473	                # or raises exception.
474	                warnings.filterwarnings(
475	                    "ignore",
476	                    "Parsing dates involving a day of month without a year specified",
477	                    DeprecationWarning,
478	                )
479	                result = dateutil_parse(text, default=default)
480	        except ParserError:
481	            result = None
482	
483	        if not result:
484	            return self.date_parse_human(
485	                text, hour=hour, minute=minute, second=second, microsecond=microsecond
486	            )
487	
488	        if (
489	            hour is None
490	            and result.hour == 5
491	            and result.minute == 5
492	            and result.second == 5
493	            and result.microsecond == 5
494	        ):
495	            return (
496	                result.replace(hour=0, minute=0, second=0, microsecond=0),
497	                result.replace(hour=23, minute=59, second=59, microsecond=999999),
498	            )
499	
500	        return result

518	    def field_name(self, field: str, suffix: str | None = None) -> str:
519	        if suffix is None:
520	            suffix = OPERATOR_MAP[self.operator]
521	
522	        if field in self.EXACT_FIELD_MAP:
523	            # Change contains to exact, do not change other (for example regex)
524	            if suffix == "substring":
525	                suffix = "iexact"
526	            return f"{self.EXACT_FIELD_MAP[field]}__{suffix}"
527	
528	        if not self.enable_fulltext and suffix == "substring":
529	            suffix = "icontains"
530	
531	        if field in self.PLAIN_FIELDS:
532	            return f"{field}__{suffix}"
533	        if field in self.STRING_FIELD_MAP:
534	            return f"{self.STRING_FIELD_MAP[field]}__{suffix}"
535	        if field in self.NONTEXT_FIELDS:
536	            if suffix not in {"substring", "iexact"}:
537	                return f"{self.NONTEXT_FIELDS[field]}__{suffix}"
538	            return self.NONTEXT_FIELDS[field]
539	        raise SearchQueryError(gettext("Unknown search field: {}").format(field))

541	    def build_field_query(
542	        self,
543	        field: str,
544	        match: Any,  # noqa: ANN401
545	        field_name: Callable[[str, str | None], str] | None = None,
546	    ) -> Q:
547	        if field_name is None:
548	            field_name = self.field_name
549	
550	        if isinstance(match, RegexExpr):
551	            # Regular expression
552	            from weblate.trans.models import Unit  # noqa: PLC0415
553	
554	            with transaction.atomic():
555	                try:
556	                    Unit.objects.annotate(test=Value("")).filter(
557	                        test__trgm_regex=match.expr
558	                    ).exists()
559	                except DataError as error:
560	                    raise SearchQueryError(
561	                        gettext("Invalid regular expression: {}").format(error)
562	                    ) from error
563	            return Q(**{field_name(field, "trgm_regex"): match.expr})
564	
565	        if isinstance(match, tuple):
566	            start, end = match
567	            # Ranges
568	            if self.operator in {":", ":="}:
569	                return Q(**{field_name(field, "range"): (start, end)})
570	            if self.operator in {":>", ":>="}:
571	                return Q(**{field_name(field, "gte"): start})
572	            return Q(**{field_name(field, "lte"): end})
573	
574	        if isinstance(match, set):
575	            return Q(**{field_name(field, "in"): match})
576	
577	        # Generic query
578	        return Q(**{field_name(field, None): match})

584	        field = self.field

586	        # Simple term based search
587	        if not field:
588	            return self.convert_non_field()
589	
590	        # Field specific code
591	        field_method: Callable[[str, dict], Q] | None = cast(
592	            "Callable[[str, dict], Q] | None",
593	            getattr(self, f"{field}_field", None),
594	        )
595	        if field_method is not None:
596	            return field_method(match, context)
597	
598	        # Field conversion
599	        convert_method = getattr(self, f"convert_{field}", None)
600	        if convert_method is not None:
601	            match = convert_method(match)
602	
603	        return self.field_extra(field, self.build_field_query(field, match), match)

The Weblate codebase consistently uses the Django ORM with parameterization, an allow-listed field map in search, and parameterized cursor.execute. The defined-but-unused PG_TRGM constants are not reachable sinks.

I've examined the primary SQLi surfaces in scope (utils/db.py, utils/search.py, lang/models.py, memory/models.py, unit ordering, screenshots ordering, api filtering paths). All use parameterization, allow-lists, or ORM Q objects. No defensible SQL injection finding.

The custom lookups (PostgreSQLSearchLookup/SubstringLookup get_rhs_op return f"ILIKE {rhs}" / f"%% {rhs} = true") use rhs = the SQL placeholder produced by process_rhs (param_pattern "%s"), not the user value; the value flows through Django's params list. PG_TRGM/PG_DROP .format() index-DDL constants are defined but I found no caller in the repo (grep across weblate/*.py shows only the definition), so they are not a reachable sink.
- weblate/utils/search.py (full): the search query language (the "q" param, also reused by addons/autotranslate.py) is parsed with pyparsing into Django ORM Q() objects. Field names are resolved through allow-list maps (PLAIN_FIELDS/NONTEXT_FIELDS/STRING_FIELD_MAP/EXACT_FIELD_MAP) with SearchQueryError on unknown fields; match values become ORM lookup values (parameterized). Repo-wide greps for cursor.execute, .raw(, RawSQL, .extra(, and f-string SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE returned only the above (plus test files). No attacker-influenced fragment reaches a raw SQL string. No defensible SQLi finding; reporting zero is the honest result.

content.text: No SQL injection findings. The Weblate codebase in scope is consistently defensive against SQLi: all raw `cursor.execute` calls use static SQL or `%s` parameter binding, the search-query language compiles to Django ORM `Q()` objects with an allow-listed field map, and all `order_by` paths validate against allow-lists or fixed dictionaries. The only string-formatted SQL (`PG_TRGM`/`PG_DROP` DDL templates in `utils/db.py`) has no caller anywhere in the repository, so it is not a reachable sink.
